abstract: Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) represent the leading non-viral platform for gene delivery. While it is well-established that physicochemical properties such as size, surface charge, and surface modifications dictate their performance, the specific impact of these attributes on transfection and distribution following intramuscular injection remains poorly understood. In this study, we synthesized a library of LNPs with varying sizes, lipid-based charges, and PEGylated lipid compositions to evaluate their behavior in vivo, using luciferase mRNA (mLuc) as a reporter. Our findings reveal that smaller, neutrally charged LNPs achieve superior transfection efficiency at the injection site. Interestingly, however, size and charge did not significantly alter the systemic distribution of the nanoparticles. We further demonstrated that PEGylated lipids with shorter acyl chains enhance transfection by facilitating more efficient cellular uptake and endosomal escape. Of note, the underlying mechanisms of cellular internalizations were found to shift depending on the acyl chain length of the PEGylated lipids. Collectively, these results highlight the critical role of nanoparticle morphology and PEG chemistry in optimizing intramuscular mRNA delivery, providing a strategic framework for the development of more potent LNP-based vaccines and therapeutics.
